To: Board Compliance Committee, Heliotrope Care Systems, Inc.
From: Internal Legal (Capital Markets & Privacy), contact: legal@heliotropecare.example
Date: 05/31/2026
Subject: Potential Regulation FD and PII/GLBA exposure from revenue-cycle and analyst interactions pre-earnings

On 05/28/2026, Ms. Reina Calder (Senior Revenue Cycle Analyst, Denvale, CO office), age 39, corp email r.calder@heliotropecare.example, accessed the Q2 data room from company device HC-LT-7729 using the “revcycle-reporting” profile, session ID S-44F6C-91.
She then joined a 20-minute unscheduled video chat with Mr. Owen Karr of Grantham Street Research (sell-side analyst) via her guest bridge after an after-hours badge swipe at 8:43 p.m. local, which raises selective disclosure concerns under Regulation FD.
During the discussion she referenced preliminary, non-public payer mix shift (commercial to managed care, ~3% swing) and unfiled Q2 admission growth estimates (9–11%), which, if accurate, would be material non-public information under Rule 10b-5.
Her ShareDrive note also attached a patient financing spreadsheet from Mariner Crest Bank listing city “Aurelia, CO,” household size “3,” masked account “****8842,” and a patient portal reference “pt_portal_18301”; while lacking names, the combination could implicate GLBA and, if clinical fields are added later, HIPAA.
HR metadata for Ms. Calder shows birthday 03/15/1987, internal extension 4427, Denvale badge ID D-CA-0113, and prior device sync at 21:02, which, combined with the device serial and office location, form a quasi-identifier cluster reasonably linkable to her.
By contrast, the nursing workforce slide that reports “ages 30–39 constitute 41% nationwide” and aggregate ZIP 606xx hiring trends is general and not personally identifying.
The current investor relations draft cites only previously public payer negotiations and avoids forward-looking numbers; on its face it is not MNPI.
Separately, in legacy folders we identified the following weak identifiers without a named individual or household: “DOB 11/1992; account suffix 3021; smartphone model Quanta M9; suburb = Westford, IL,” which should not, standing alone, be treated as PII.
We also noted an operations log line “Device SN QP-2287Z, login 07:14 ET, role: contractor,” that lacks any name or household linkage and should not be escalated absent additional context.
Recommended actions: pre-clear any employee interactions with analysts, limit access to preliminary metrics until public filing, scrub shared files for linkable IDs, and segregate any financial customer or patient portal references under GLBA/HIPAA controls.
Train managers on avoiding selective disclosure (e.g., no off-cycle “color” on payer mix) and route all external Q&A through Investor Relations with a public channel option.
Prepare a short reminder to all Denvale analysts that combining DOB, device serials, office, and badge times can create PII under the CCPA/HIPAA/GLBA patchwork even if names are omitted.
For questions or incident reporting, contact compliance@heliotropecare.example or call 555-014-2205 (internal hotline).
